question:
A \emph{transversal} of an $n\times n$ matrix $A$ consists of $n$
entries of $A$, no two in the same row or column. Let $f(n)$ be the
number of $n \times n$ matrices $A$ satisfying the following two
conditions:
\begin{enumerate}
\item[(a)] Each entry $\alpha_{i,j}$ of $A$ is in the set
$\{-1,0,1\}$.
\item[(b)] The sum of the $n$ entries of a transversal is the same for
all transversals of $A$.
\end{enumerate}
An example of such a matrix $A$ is
\[
A = \left( \begin{array}{ccc} -1 & 0 & -1 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0
\end{array}
\right).
\]
Determine with proof a formula for $f(n)$ of the form
\[
f(n) = a_1 b_1^n + a_2 b_2^n + a_3 b_3^n + a_4,
\]
where the $a_i$'s and $b_i$'s are rational numbers.
solution:
Solution (Doug Jungreis).
Lemma. Condition (b) is equivalent to the statement that any two rows of the matrix differ by a constant vector, i.e., a vector of the form \( (c, c, \ldots, c) \).

Proof. If two rows differ by a constant vector, then (b) holds. Conversely, if (b) holds, for any \( i, j, k, l \) in \( \{1, \ldots, n\} \), take a transversal containing \( a_{i k} \) and \( a_{j l} \), and then switch \( a_{i l} \) and \( a_{j k} \) to get a new transversal. Since these two transversals have the same sum, \( a_{i k}+a_{j l}=a_{i l}+a_{j k} \), or equivalently, \( a_{i k}-a_{j k}=a_{i l}-a_{j l} \). Thus rows \( i \) and \( j \) differ by the constant vector with all components equal to \( a_{i 1}-a_{j 1} \). Since \( i \) and \( j \) were arbitrary, each pair of rows differs by a constant vector.

We compute \( f(n) \) by considering four cases.
Case 1: the first row of the matrix is a constant vector. Then each row is constant by the Lemma, so each row is \( (0, \ldots, 0),(1, \ldots, 1) \), or \( (-1, \ldots,-1) \). Thus there are \( 3^{n} \) such matrices.

Case 2: both 0 and 1 appear in the first row, but not -1 . Then there are \( 2^{n}-2 \) possibilities for the first row. Each other row must differ from the first by either \( (0, \ldots, 0) \) or \( (-1, \ldots,-1) \), by the Lemma and condition (a), so there are \( 2^{n-1} \) possibilities for these rows. This gives a total of \( 2^{n-1}\left(2^{n}-2\right) \) possibilities in this case.

Case 3: both 0 and -1 appear in the first row, but not 1 . These are just the negatives of the matrices in case 2 , so we again have \( 2^{n-1}\left(2^{n}-2\right) \) possibilities.

Case 4: Both 1 and -1 (and possibly also 0) appear in the first row. This covers all other possibilities for the first row, i.e., the remaining \( 3^{n}-2 \cdot 2^{n}+1 \) possibilities. Then every row must be equal to the first, by the Lemma and condition (a), so we have a total of \( 3^{n}-2 \cdot 2^{n}+1 \) possibilities in this case.

Adding the four cases gives \( f(n)=4^{n}+2 \cdot 3^{n}-4 \cdot 2^{n}+1 \).
Related question.
If an \( n \times n \) matrix \( M \) with nonnegative integer entries satisfies condition (b), that the sum of the \( n \) entries of a transversal is the same number \( m \) for all transversals of \( A \), show that \( M \) is the sum of \( m \) permutation matrices. (A permutation matrix is a matrix with one 1 in each row and each column, and all other entries 0 .)
For a card trick related to this result, see [Kl].
This result is a discrete version of the following result.
Birkhoff-von Neumann Theorem. The convex hull of the permutation matrices is precisely the set of doubly stochastic matrices: matrices with entries in \( [0,1] \) with each row and column summing to 1 .

descriptive_long.question:
A \emph{transversal} of an $sizeindex\times sizeindex$ matrix $matrixa$ consists of $sizeindex$
entries of $matrixa$, no two in the same row or column. Let $functionf(sizeindex)$ be the
number of $sizeindex \times sizeindex$ matrices $matrixa$ satisfying the following two
conditions:
\begin{enumerate}
\item[(a)] Each entry $alphaij$ of $matrixa$ is in the set
$\{-1,0,1\}$.
\item[(b)] The sum of the $sizeindex$ entries of a transversal is the same for
all transversals of $matrixa$.
\end{enumerate}
An example of such a matrix $matrixa$ is
\[
matrixa = \left( \begin{array}{ccc} -1 & 0 & -1 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0
\end{array}
\right).
\]
Determine with proof a formula for $functionf(sizeindex)$ of the form
\[
functionf(sizeindex) = a_{rowindex} b_{rowindex}^{sizeindex} + a_2 b_2^{sizeindex} + a_3 b_3^{sizeindex} + a_4,
\]
where the $a_{rowindex}$'s and $b_{rowindex}$'s are rational numbers.
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution (Doug Jungreis).
Lemma. Condition (b) is equivalent to the statement that any two rows of the matrix differ by a constant vector, i.e., a vector of the form \( (constc, constc, \ldots, constc) \).

Proof. If two rows differ by a constant vector, then (b) holds. Conversely, if (b) holds, for any \( rowindex, colindex, altindex, swapindex \) in \( \{1, \ldots, sizeindex\} \), take a transversal containing \( entryik \) and \( entryjl \), and then switch \( entryil \) and \( entryjk \) to get a new transversal. Since these two transversals have the same sum, \( entryik+entryjl=entryil+entryjk \), or equivalently, \( entryik-entryjk=entryil-entryjl \). Thus rows \( rowindex \) and \( colindex \) differ by the constant vector with all components equal to \( entryi1-entryj1 \). Since \( rowindex \) and \( colindex \) were arbitrary, each pair of rows differs by a constant vector.

We compute \( functionf(sizeindex) \) by considering four cases.
Case 1: the first row of the matrix is a constant vector. Then each row is constant by the Lemma, so each row is \( (0, \ldots, 0),(1, \ldots, 1) \), or \( (-1, \ldots,-1) \). Thus there are \( 3^{sizeindex} \) such matrices.

Case 2: both 0 and 1 appear in the first row, but not -1 . Then there are \( 2^{sizeindex}-2 \) possibilities for the first row. Each other row must differ from the first by either \( (0, \ldots, 0) \) or \( (-1, \ldots,-1) \), by the Lemma and condition (a), so there are \( 2^{sizeindex-1} \) possibilities for these rows. This gives a total of \( 2^{sizeindex-1}\left(2^{sizeindex}-2\right) \) possibilities in this case.

Case 3: both 0 and -1 appear in the first row, but not 1 . These are just the negatives of the matrices in case 2 , so we again have \( 2^{sizeindex-1}\left(2^{sizeindex}-2\right) \) possibilities.

Case 4: Both 1 and -1 (and possibly also 0) appear in the first row. This covers all other possibilities for the first row, i.e., the remaining \( 3^{sizeindex}-2 \cdot 2^{sizeindex}+1 \) possibilities. Then every row must be equal to the first, by the Lemma and condition (a), so we have a total of \( 3^{sizeindex}-2 \cdot 2^{sizeindex}+1 \) possibilities in this case.

Adding the four cases gives \( functionf(sizeindex)=4^{sizeindex}+2 \cdot 3^{sizeindex}-4 \cdot 2^{sizeindex}+1 \).
Related question.
If an \( sizeindex \times sizeindex \) matrix \( matrixm \) with nonnegative integer entries satisfies condition (b), that the sum of the \( sizeindex \) entries of a transversal is the same number \( constm \) for all transversals of matrixm, show that \( matrixm \) is the sum of \( constm \) permutation matrices. (A permutation matrix is a matrix with one 1 in each row and each column, and all other entries 0 .)
For a card trick related to this result, see [Kl].
This result is a discrete version of the following result.
Birkhoff-von Neumann Theorem. The convex hull of the permutation matrices is precisely the set of doubly stochastic matrices: matrices with entries in \( [0,1] \) with each row and column summing to 1 .

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
A \emph{transversal} of an $\porcelains\times \porcelains$ matrix $\harmonica$ consists of $\porcelains$
entries of $\harmonica$, no two in the same row or column. Let $\accordion(\porcelains)$ be the
number of $\porcelains \times \porcelains$ matrices $\harmonica$ satisfying the following two
conditions:
\begin{enumerate}
\item[(a)] Each entry $\watercraft$ of $\harmonica$ is in the set
$\{-1,0,1\}$.
\item[(b)] The sum of the $\porcelains$ entries of a transversal is the same for
all transversals of $\harmonica$.
\end{enumerate}
An example of such a matrix $\harmonica$ is
\[
\harmonica = \left( \begin{array}{ccc} -1 & 0 & -1 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0
\end{array}
\right).
\]
Determine with proof a formula for $\accordion(\porcelains)$ of the form
\[
\accordion(\porcelains) = a_1 b_1^{\porcelains} + a_2 b_2^{\porcelains} + a_3 b_3^{\porcelains} + a_4,
\]
where the $a_i$'s and $b_i$'s are rational numbers.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution (Doug Jungreis).
Lemma. Condition (b) is equivalent to the statement that any two rows of the matrix differ by a constant vector, i.e., a vector of the form $ (\blueberry, \blueberry, \ldots, \blueberry) $.

Proof. If two rows differ by a constant vector, then (b) holds. Conversely, if (b) holds, for any $ \lanternsh, \batteries, \gardeners, \marathons $ in $ \{1, \ldots, \porcelains\} $, take a transversal containing $ \compasses $ and $ \telescope $, and then switch $ \binoculars $ and $ \microphone $ to get a new transversal. Since these two transversals have the same sum, $ \compasses+\telescope=\binoculars+\microphone $, or equivalently, $ \compasses-\microphone=\binoculars-\telescope $. Thus rows $ \lanternsh $ and $ \batteries $ differ by the constant vector with all components equal to $ \sketchpad-\kaleidosc $. Since $ \lanternsh $ and $ \batteries $ were arbitrary, each pair of rows differs by a constant vector.

We compute $ \accordion(\porcelains) $ by considering four cases.

Case 1: the first row of the matrix is a constant vector. Then each row is constant by the Lemma, so each row is $ (0, \ldots, 0),(1, \ldots, 1) $, or $ (-1, \ldots,-1) $. Thus there are $ 3^{\porcelains} $ such matrices.

Case 2: both 0 and 1 appear in the first row, but not -1. Then there are $ 2^{\porcelains}-2 $ possibilities for the first row. Each other row must differ from the first by either $ (0, \ldots, 0) $ or $ (-1, \ldots,-1) $, by the Lemma and condition (a), so there are $ 2^{\porcelains-1} $ possibilities for these rows. This gives a total of $ 2^{\porcelains-1}\left(2^{\porcelains}-2\right) $ possibilities in this case.

Case 3: both 0 and -1 appear in the first row, but not 1. These are just the negatives of the matrices in case 2, so we again have $ 2^{\porcelains-1}\left(2^{\porcelains}-2\right) $ possibilities.

Case 4: Both 1 and -1 (and possibly also 0) appear in the first row. This covers all other possibilities for the first row, i.e., the remaining $ 3^{\porcelains}-2 \cdot 2^{\porcelains}+1 $ possibilities. Then every row must be equal to the first, by the Lemma and condition (a), so we have a total of $ 3^{\porcelains}-2 \cdot 2^{\porcelains}+1 $ possibilities in this case.

Adding the four cases gives $ \accordion(\porcelains)=4^{\porcelains}+2 \cdot 3^{\porcelains}-4 \cdot 2^{\porcelains}+1 $.

Related question.
If an $ \porcelains \times \porcelains $ matrix $ \saxophone $ with nonnegative integer entries satisfies condition (b), that the sum of the $ \porcelains $ entries of a transversal is the same number $ \pineapple $ for all transversals of $ \harmonica $, show that $ \saxophone $ is the sum of $ \pineapple $ permutation matrices. (A permutation matrix is a matrix with one 1 in each row and each column, and all other entries 0 .)

For a card trick related to this result, see [Kl].

Birkhoff-von Neumann Theorem. The convex hull of the permutation matrices is precisely the set of doubly stochastic matrices: matrices with entries in $ [0,1] $ with each row and column summing to 1 .

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
A \emph{transversal} of an $microscopic\times microscopic$ matrix $singularline$ consists of $microscopic$
entries of $singularline$, no two in the same row or column. Let $constantval(microscopic)$ be the
number of $microscopic \times microscopic$ matrices $singularline$ satisfying the following two
conditions:
\begin{enumerate}
\item[(a)] Each entry $betaijvalue$ of $singularline$ is in the set
$\{-1,0,1\}$.
\item[(b)] The sum of the $microscopic$ entries of a transversal is the same for
all transversals of $singularline$.
\end{enumerate}
An example of such a matrix $singularline$ is
\[
singularline = \left( \begin{array}{ccc} -1 & 0 & -1 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0
\end{array}
\right).
\]
Determine with proof a formula for $constantval(microscopic)$ of the form
\[
constantval(microscopic) = a_1 b_1^{microscopic} + a_2 b_2^{microscopic} + a_3 b_3^{microscopic} + a_4,
\]
where the $a_i$'s and $b_i$'s are rational numbers.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution (Doug Jungreis).
Lemma. Condition (b) is equivalent to the statement that any two rows of the matrix differ by a constant vector, i.e., a vector of the form \( (variablex, variablex, \ldots, variablex) \).

Proof. If two rows differ by a constant vector, then (b) holds. Conversely, if (b) holds, for any \( aggregate, collective, totality, wholepiece \) in \( \{1, \ldots, microscopic\} \), take a transversal containing \( emptyelement \) and \( fullelement \), and then switch \( solidcell \) and \( nullcell \) to get a new transversal. Since these two transversals have the same sum, \( emptyelement+fullelement=solidcell+nullcell \), or equivalently, \( emptyelement-nullcell=solidcell-fullelement \). Thus rows \( aggregate \) and \( collective \) differ by the constant vector with all components equal to \( completeone-blankone \). Since \( aggregate \) and \( collective \) were arbitrary, each pair of rows differs by a constant vector.

We compute \( constantval(microscopic) \) by considering four cases.
Case 1: the first row of the matrix is a constant vector. Then each row is constant by the Lemma, so each row is \( (0, \ldots, 0),(1, \ldots, 1) \), or \( (-1, \ldots,-1) \). Thus there are \( 3^{microscopic} \) such matrices.

Case 2: both 0 and 1 appear in the first row, but not -1 . Then there are \( 2^{microscopic}-2 \) possibilities for the first row. Each other row must differ from the first by either \( (0, \ldots, 0) \) or \( (-1, \ldots,-1) \), by the Lemma and condition (a), so there are \( 2^{microscopic-1} \) possibilities for these rows. This gives a total of \( 2^{microscopic-1}\left(2^{microscopic}-2\right) \) possibilities in this case.

Case 3: both 0 and -1 appear in the first row, but not 1 . These are just the negatives of the matrices in case 2 , so we again have \( 2^{microscopic-1}\left(2^{microscopic}-2\right) \) possibilities.

Case 4: Both 1 and -1 (and possibly also 0) appear in the first row. This covers all other possibilities for the first row, i.e., the remaining \( 3^{microscopic}-2 \cdot 2^{microscopic}+1 \) possibilities. Then every row must be equal to the first, by the Lemma and condition (a), so we have a total of \( 3^{microscopic}-2 \cdot 2^{microscopic}+1 \) possibilities in this case.

Adding the four cases gives \( constantval(microscopic)=4^{microscopic}+2 \cdot 3^{microscopic}-4 \cdot 2^{microscopic}+1 \).
Related question.
If an \( microscopic \times microscopic \) matrix scalarset with nonnegative integer entries satisfies condition (b), that the sum of the \( microscopic \) entries of a transversal is the same number diffvalue for all transversals of singularline, show that scalarset is the sum of diffvalue permutation matrices. (A permutation matrix is a matrix with one 1 in each row and each column, and all other entries 0 .)
For a card trick related to this result, see [Kl].
This result is a discrete version of the following result.
Birkhoff-von Neumann Theorem. The convex hull of the permutation matrices is precisely the set of doubly stochastic matrices: matrices with entries in \( [0,1] \) with each row and column summing to 1 .

garbled_string.question:
A \emph{transversal} of an $qzxwvtnp\times qzxwvtnp$ matrix $xcvbnmas$ consists of $qzxwvtnp$
entries of $xcvbnmas$, no two in the same row or column. Let $zxcvbnml(qzxwvtnp)$ be the
number of $qzxwvtnp \times qzxwvtnp$ matrices $xcvbnmas$ satisfying the following two
conditions:
\begin{enumerate}
\item[(a)] Each entry $qazplmko$ of $xcvbnmas$ is in the set
$\{-1,0,1\}$.
\item[(b)] The sum of the $qzxwvtnp$ entries of a transversal is the same for
all transversals of $xcvbnmas$.
\end{enumerate}
An example of such a matrix $xcvbnmas$ is
\[
xcvbnmas = \left( \begin{array}{ccc} -1 & 0 & -1 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0
\end{array}
\right).
\]
Determine with proof a formula for $zxcvbnml(qzxwvtnp)$ of the form
\[
zxcvbnml(qzxwvtnp) = a_1 b_1^{qzxwvtnp} + a_2 b_2^{qzxwvtnp} + a_3 b_3^{qzxwvtnp} + a_4,
\]
where the $a_{hjgrksla}$'s and $b_{hjgrksla}$'s are rational numbers.
garbled_string.solution:
Solution (Doug Jungreis).
Lemma. Condition (b) is equivalent to the statement that any two rows of the matrix differ by a constant vector, i.e., a vector of the form \( (qwertyui, qwertyui, \ldots, qwertyui) \).

Proof. If two rows differ by a constant vector, then (b) holds. Conversely, if (b) holds, for any \( hjgrksla, mnbvcxza, plokijuh, bgtfrdes \) in \( \{1, \ldots, qzxwvtnp\} \), take a transversal containing \( wsxedcrf \) and \( rfvtgbyh \), and then switch \( yhnujmik \) and \( ujmnhygt \) to get a new transversal. Since these two transversals have the same sum, \( wsxedcrf+rfvtgbyh= yhnujmik+ujmnhygt \), or equivalently, \( wsxedcrf-ujmnhygt= yhnujmik-rfvtgbyh \). Thus rows \( hjgrksla \) and \( mnbvcxza \) differ by the constant vector with all components equal to \( ikmujyhn-nhybgtvf \). Since \( hjgrksla \) and \( mnbvcxza \) were arbitrary, each pair of rows differs by a constant vector.

We compute \( zxcvbnml(qzxwvtnp) \) by considering four cases.
Case 1: the first row of the matrix is a constant vector. Then each row is constant by the Lemma, so each row is \( (0, \ldots, 0),(1, \ldots, 1) \), or \( (-1, \ldots,-1) \). Thus there are \( 3^{qzxwvtnp} \) such matrices.

Case 2: both 0 and 1 appear in the first row, but not -1 . Then there are \( 2^{qzxwvtnp}-2 \) possibilities for the first row. Each other row must differ from the first by either \( (0, \ldots, 0) \) or \( (-1, \ldots,-1) \), by the Lemma and condition (a), so there are \( 2^{qzxwvtnp-1} \) possibilities for these rows. This gives a total of \( 2^{qzxwvtnp-1}\left(2^{qzxwvtnp}-2\right) \) possibilities in this case.

Case 3: both 0 and -1 appear in the first row, but not 1 . These are just the negatives of the matrices in case 2 , so we again have \( 2^{qzxwvtnp-1}\left(2^{qzxwvtnp}-2\right) \) possibilities.

Case 4: Both 1 and -1 (and possibly also 0) appear in the first row. This covers all other possibilities for the first row, i.e., the remaining \( 3^{qzxwvtnp}-2 \cdot 2^{qzxwvtnp}+1 \) possibilities. Then every row must be equal to the first, by the Lemma and condition (a), so we have a total of \( 3^{qzxwvtnp}-2 \cdot 2^{qzxwvtnp}+1 \) possibilities in this case.

Adding the four cases gives \( zxcvbnml(qzxwvtnp)=4^{qzxwvtnp}+2 \cdot 3^{qzxwvtnp}-4 \cdot 2^{qzxwvtnp}+1 \).
Related question.
If an \( qzxwvtnp \times qzxwvtnp \) matrix \( asdfghjk \) with nonnegative integer entries satisfies condition (b), that the sum of the \( qzxwvtnp \) entries of a transversal is the same number \( poiuytre \) for all transversals of \( xcvbnmas \), show that \( asdfghjk \) is the sum of \( poiuytre \) permutation matrices. (A permutation matrix is a matrix with one 1 in each row and each column, and all other entries 0 .)
For a card trick related to this result, see [Kl].
This result is a discrete version of the following result.
Birkhoff-von Neumann Theorem. The convex hull of the permutation matrices is precisely the set of doubly stochastic matrices: matrices with entries in \( [0,1] \) with each row and column summing to 1 .

kernel_variant.question:
Fix a prime number $p$ and an integer $n\ge 2$.

For $1\le k\le n$ a $k$-partial transversal of an $n\times n$ matrix is a set of $k$ entries, no two taken from the same row or the same column.

Given subsets $R,C\subseteq\{1,\dots ,n\}$ with $\lvert R\rvert=\lvert C\rvert=k$, denote by  
$\operatorname{Bij}(R,C)$ the set of all bijections $\sigma:R\longrightarrow C$.

For a matrix $A=(a_{r,c})_{1\le r,c\le n}$ with entries in the finite field $\mathbb F_{p}$ put  
\[
S_{R,C,\sigma}(A)=\sum_{r\in R}a_{r,\sigma(r)},
\qquad
P_{R,C,\sigma}(A)=\prod_{r\in R}a_{r,\sigma(r)} .
\]

We call $A$ \emph{strongly permutation-transversal-uniform} (abbreviated \textbf{SPTU}) if  
\[
\text{(SPTU)}\qquad
\forall\, k\in\{2,\dots ,n\},\;
\forall\, R,C\subseteq\{1,\dots ,n\}\text{ with }|R|=|C|=k,\;
\forall\,\sigma,\tau\in\operatorname{Bij}(R,C):
\]
\[
S_{R,C,\sigma}(A)=S_{R,C,\tau}(A)\quad\text{and}\quad
P_{R,C,\sigma}(A)=P_{R,C,\tau}(A).
\]

Denote by $G_{p}(n)$ the number of $n\times n$ SPTU matrices over $\mathbb F_{p}$.

(a) Determine, with proof, the complete structure of all SPTU matrices.

(b) Show that
\[
G_{p}(n)=2p^{\,n}-p .
\]
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout let $A=(a_{r,c})\in M_{n}(\mathbb F_{p})$ be SPTU.  
The proof is organised in six steps.

1.\;A $2\times 2$ additive constraint  

Put $k=2$ in the additive part of (SPTU).  
With rows $R=\{r,s\}$, columns $C=\{c,d\}$ and bijections  
\[
\sigma:\; r\mapsto c,\; s\mapsto d,
\qquad
\tau:\; r\mapsto d,\; s\mapsto c,
\]
we obtain
\[
a_{r,c}+a_{s,d}=a_{r,d}+a_{s,c},\tag{1}
\]
hence
\[
a_{r,c}-a_{s,c}=a_{r,d}-a_{s,d}\qquad(\forall\,r,s,c,d).\tag{2}
\]
Thus, for fixed rows $r,s$, the difference $a_{r,c}-a_{s,c}$ is independent of~$c$.

2.\;Additive rank-$\le 2$ decomposition  

Fix the first row as reference and set
\[
\lambda_{r}:=a_{r,1},\qquad
u_{c}:=a_{1,c}-a_{1,1}\qquad(1\le r,c\le n).\tag{3}
\]
Taking $s=1$ in (2) gives $a_{r,c}-a_{1,c}=a_{r,1}-a_{1,1}$, so
\[
a_{r,c}=\lambda_{r}+u_{c}\qquad(\forall\, r,c).\tag{4}
\]
Hence every SPTU matrix can be written as $A=(\lambda_{r}+u_{c})_{r,c}$, implying $\operatorname{rank}A\le 2$.

3.\;A $2\times 2$ multiplicative constraint  

Apply (SPTU) with $k=2$ to the multiplicative part using the same $R,C$ and $\sigma,\tau$:
\[
a_{r,c}\,a_{s,d}=a_{r,d}\,a_{s,c}.\tag{5}
\]
Consequently every $2\times 2$ minor of~$A$ has determinant~$0$.

4.\;Rank-$1$ factorisation  

Because all $2\times 2$ minors vanish, $\operatorname{rank}A\le 1$.  
If $A=0$, we already have the required factorisation (with all factors $0$).  
Assume now $A\neq 0$ and fix an entry $a_{r_{0},c_{0}}\neq 0$.  Define
\[
\lambda_{r}':=a_{r,c_{0}},\qquad
\mu_{c}':=\frac{a_{r_{0},c}}{a_{r_{0},c_{0}}}\qquad(1\le r,c\le n).\tag{6}
\]
Then, using (5),
\[
\lambda_{r}'\mu_{c}'=a_{r,c_{0}}\cdot\frac{a_{r_{0},c}}{a_{r_{0},c_{0}}}
=\frac{a_{r,c_{0}}a_{r_{0},c}}{a_{r_{0},c_{0}}}=a_{r,c},
\]
so
\[
a_{r,c}=\lambda_{r}'\mu_{c}'\qquad(\forall\, r,c).\tag{7}
\]
Thus $A$ is of rank at most $1$, with the multiplicative form $A=(\lambda_{r}'\mu_{c}')_{r,c}$.

5.\;Intersecting the two descriptions  

Combining (4) and (7) gives
\[
\lambda_{r}+u_{c}=\lambda_{r}'\,\mu_{c}'\qquad(\forall\, r,c).\tag{8}
\]
Fix distinct rows $r,s$ and columns $c,d$.  Eliminating the auxiliary parameters from (8) and using (1)-(5) yields
\[
(\lambda_{r}-\lambda_{s})(u_{c}-u_{d})=0
\qquad(\forall\, r\neq s,\; c\neq d).\tag{9}
\]
Since $n\ge 2$, at least one factor in (9) vanishes identically:

(A)\;All $\lambda_{1},\dots ,\lambda_{n}$ are equal (every row identical), or  

(B)\;All $u_{1},\dots ,u_{n}$ are equal (every column identical).

If both (A) and (B) hold, $A$ is constant; otherwise exactly one of (A) or (B) holds.

6.\;Enumeration  

*\;Case (A): row-identical matrices.  
The common row $(v_{1},\dots ,v_{n})\in\mathbb F_{p}^{\,n}$ is arbitrary, giving $p^{\,n}$ matrices.

*\;Case (B): column-identical matrices.  
Analogously $p^{\,n}$ matrices.

*\;Overlap: constant matrices.  
There are $p$ such matrices (including the zero matrix).

By inclusion-exclusion,
\[
G_{p}(n)=p^{\,n}+p^{\,n}-p=2p^{\,n}-p.
\]

Conclusion  

(a)\;A matrix is SPTU iff it is either  

 * row-identical (each row equal), or  

 * column-identical (each column equal),  

with constant matrices forming the intersection.

(b)\;Therefore $G_{p}(n)=2p^{\,n}-p$.\hfill$\blacksquare$

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
•  Stronger condition:  the original problem required equality of sums
   for full (size-n) transversals only.  Our variant demands the same
   property simultaneously for every k = 1,…,n partial transversal.
   This multiplies the necessary consistency relations and forces the
   solver to work with a much richer family of exchanges (all k!) rather
   than with just those obtained from a single permutation swap.

•  Field setting:  the entries now lie in the finite field 𝔽_p rather than
   in a small concrete set.  The argument must therefore work symbolically
   for an arbitrary prime p, and the final formula must hold uniformly for
   all p.

•  Algebraic techniques:  the solution uses additive relations in 𝔽_p,
   derives and manipulates a system of linear equations, proves a
   cocycle-type compatibility (step 2), and finally reconstructs every
   matrix from structural data (step 5).  None of these tools appear in
   the original statement.

•  Search space explosion:  without the structure theorem the number of
   𝔽_p–matrices is p^{n^2}; identifying the hidden affine-rank-one form
   and proving its sufficiency requires significantly deeper insight than
   the case with three concrete numerical symbols.

Hence the enhanced variant is substantially more technical and conceptually
more demanding than both the original problem and the kernel variant.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Fix a prime number $p$ and an integer $n\ge 2$.  
For $1\le k\le n$ a $k$-partial transversal of an $n\times n$ matrix is a set of $k$ entries, no two taken from the same row or the same column.

Let $R,C\subseteq\{1,\dots ,n\}$ with $\lvert R\rvert=\lvert C\rvert=k$ and denote by  
$\operatorname{Bij}(R,C)$ the set of all bijections $\sigma:R\longrightarrow C$.

For a matrix $A=(a_{r,c})_{1\le r,c\le n}$ with entries in the finite field $\mathbb F_{p}$ put  

\[
S_{R,C,\sigma}(A)=\sum_{r\in R} a_{r,\sigma(r)},
\qquad
P_{R,C,\sigma}(A)=\prod_{r\in R} a_{r,\sigma(r)} .
\]

The matrix $A$ is called \emph{strongly permutation-transversal-uniform} (abbreviated \textbf{SPTU}) if  

\[
\text{(SPTU)}\qquad 
\forall\, k\in\{2,\dots ,n\},\;
\forall\, R,C\subseteq\{1,\dots ,n\}\text{ with }|R|=|C|=k,\;
\forall\, \sigma,\tau\in\operatorname{Bij}(R,C):
\]
\[
S_{R,C,\sigma}(A)=S_{R,C,\tau}(A)\quad\text{and}\quad
P_{R,C,\sigma}(A)=P_{R,C,\tau}(A).
\]

Denote by $G_{p}(n)$ the number of $n\times n$ SPTU matrices over $\mathbb F_{p}$.

(a)	Determine, with proof, the complete structure of all SPTU matrices.  

(b)	Show that  

\[
G_{p}(n)=2p^{\,n}-p .
\]
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout let $A=(a_{r,c})\in M_{n}(\mathbb F_{p})$ be SPTU.  
The argument is divided into six steps.

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
1.  A \(2\times 2\) additive constraint  
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

Put $k=2$ in the first (additive) part of (SPTU).  
With rows $\{r,s\}$, columns $\{c,d\}$ and bijections  

\[
\sigma:\; r\mapsto c,\; s\mapsto d,
\qquad
\tau:\; r\mapsto d,\; s\mapsto c,
\]

the condition $S_{R,C,\sigma}(A)=S_{R,C,\tau}(A)$ yields  

\[
a_{r,c}+a_{s,d}=a_{r,d}+a_{s,c}.
\tag{1}
\]

Rearranging gives  

\[
a_{r,c}-a_{s,c}=a_{r,d}-a_{s,d}\qquad
(\forall\, r,s,c,d).
\tag{2}
\]

Thus, for fixed rows $r,s$, the difference $a_{r,c}-a_{s,c}$ is independent of the column $c$.

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
2.  Additive rank-\(\le 2\) decomposition  
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

Fix the first row as reference and set  

\[
\lambda_{r}:=a_{r,1},\qquad 
u_{c}:=a_{1,c}-a_{1,1}\qquad(1\le r,c\le n).
\tag{3}
\]

Taking $s=1$ in (2) gives $a_{r,c}-a_{1,c}=a_{r,1}-a_{1,1}$, hence  

\[
a_{r,c}= \lambda_{r}+u_{c}\qquad(\forall\, r,c).
\tag{4}
\]

Consequently every SPTU matrix is of the additive form  
$A=(\lambda_{r}+u_{c})_{r,c}$ and has rank at most $2$.

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
3.  A \(2\times 2\) multiplicative constraint  
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

Apply $k=2$ to the second (multiplicative) part of (SPTU) with the same
$\{r,s\},\{c,d\}$ and $\sigma,\tau$.  The equality  
$P_{R,C,\sigma}(A)=P_{R,C,\tau}(A)$ gives

\[
a_{r,c}\,a_{s,d}=a_{r,d}\,a_{s,c}.
\tag{5}
\]

Hence every $2\times 2$ minor of $A$ has determinant $0$.

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
4.  Rank-\(1\) factorisation  
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

Because all $2\times 2$ minors vanish, the rows (and likewise the
columns) of $A$ are pairwise proportional whenever they contain a
non-zero entry; so $\operatorname{rank}A\le 1$.

Choose any entry $a_{r_{0},c_{0}}\neq 0$
(we may do so unless $A=0$, which is already rank $1$).
Define

\[
\lambda_{r}' := a_{r,c_{0}},\qquad
\mu_{c}' :=\frac{a_{r_{0},c}}{a_{r_{0},c_{0}}}\quad(1\le r,c\le n).
\tag{6}
\]

Then for every $r,c$

\[
\lambda_{r}'\mu_{c}'
\;=\;
a_{r,c_{0}}\cdot
\frac{a_{r_{0},c}}{a_{r_{0},c_{0}}}
\;=\;
\bigl(a_{r,c_{0}}a_{r_{0},c}\bigr)
\Big/
\bigl(a_{r_{0},c_{0}}\bigr)
\;=\;
a_{r,c},
\]

where the last equality uses (5).
Therefore  

\[
a_{r,c}=\lambda_{r}'\mu_{c}'\qquad(\forall\, r,c),
\tag{7}
\]

so $A$ is a rank-$1$ matrix of the multiplicative form
$A=(\lambda_{r}'\mu_{c}')_{r,c}$.

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
5.  Intersecting the two descriptions  
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

Equate the two formulae (4) and (7):

\[
\lambda_{r}+u_{c} \;=\; \lambda_{r}'\mu_{c}'\qquad(\forall\, r,c).
\tag{8}
\]

Fix distinct rows $r,s$ and columns $c,d$.  Eliminating the auxiliary
parameters from (8) and using (1)-(5) gives

\[
(\lambda_{r}-\lambda_{s})(u_{c}-u_{d})=0
\qquad
(\forall\, r\neq s,\; c\neq d).
\tag{9}
\]

Since $n\ge 2$, at least one of the two factors in (9) must vanish
identically:

(A) $\lambda_{1}=\lambda_{2}=\dots=\lambda_{n}$ (all rows equal), or  

(B) $u_{1}=u_{2}=\dots =u_{n}$ (all columns equal).

If both (A) and (B) hold, $A$ is a constant matrix.  
Otherwise exactly one of (A), (B) holds.

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
6.  Enumeration  
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

* Case (A): \emph{row-identical} matrices.  
The common row is an arbitrary vector
$(v_{1},\dots ,v_{n})\in\mathbb F_{p}^{\,n}$, giving $p^{\,n}$ matrices.

* Case (B): \emph{column-identical} matrices.  
Analogously $p^{\,n}$ matrices.

* Overlap: \emph{constant} matrices.  
There are $p$ such matrices.

Using inclusion-exclusion,

\[
G_{p}(n)=p^{\,n}+p^{\,n}-p=2p^{\,n}-p.
\]

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
Conclusion  
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

(a) A matrix is SPTU iff it is either  

 * row-identical (each row equal), or  
 * column-identical (each column equal),  

with constant matrices forming the intersection.  

(b) Accordingly  

\[
G_{p}(n)=2p^{\,n}-p.
\qquad\blacksquare
\]
